source: ICO; UK judiciary; Big Brother Watch; Liberty; Ada Lovelace Institute
notes: Regulatory enforcement actions, court cases, and parliamentary accountability records related to facial recognition systems in the UK. These are not geographic deployment records — see data/deployments/ for location data.

actions.case_name: R (Bridges) v Chief Constable of South Wales Police
actions.respondent: Chief Constable of South Wales Police
actions.outcome: Appeal allowed — AFR use found unlawful
key_findings: AFR Locate violated Article 8 ECHR (right to privacy)
Use violated UK Data Protection Act 2018
Police had excessive discretion in compiling watchlists
Public Sector Equality Duty not properly considered
Potential for discriminatory effect not adequately assessed
actions.significance: First successful legal challenge to automated facial recognition in the UK. Landmark ruling on surveillance legality. Bridges was captured in two deployments: Queen Street Cardiff (21 Dec 2017) and Motorpoint Arena Cardiff (27 Mar 2018).
actions.notes: South Wales Police, not Metropolitan Police. Included as precedent directly relevant to Met LFR legal basis.

actions.claimant: Shaun Thompson; Silkie Carlo (Big Brother Watch)
actions.respondent: Commissioner of Police of the Metropolis
actions.outcome: Challenge dismissed — Met LFR policy held lawful
key_findings: Met's LFR policy does not breach human rights law
actions.significance: Challenge brought partly on basis of Shaun Thompson misidentification (Feb 2024, Liverpool Street area — see lfr-006). First major High Court test of Met LFR policy.

actions.case_name: ICO Enforcement Notice — Clearview AI Inc
actions.outcome: £7,552,800 fine; ordered to stop processing UK residents' data; ordered to delete all UK resident data
actions.appeal_status: Upper Tribunal upheld ICO appeal Oct 2025 on 3 of 4 grounds; Clearview granted permission to appeal to Court of Appeal Dec 2025
actions.notes: Clearview scrapes public images from the web and social media to build a facial recognition database. ICO found this violated UK GDPR. First-tier Tribunal initially overturned (Oct 2023) on jurisdiction grounds; Upper Tribunal reversed Oct 2025, finding Clearview 'related to monitoring UK residents'. Not a London deployment — included as regulatory action affecting London residents.

actions.actor: Serco Leisure; Serco Jersey; 7 associated leisure trusts
actions.outcome: Ordered to stop all biometric processing for employee attendance; destroy all biometric data within 3 months
actions.notes: 2,000+ employees at 38 leisure facilities subjected to facial recognition and fingerprint scanning for attendance monitoring. ICO found unlawful biometric data processing. Includes London facilities. Relevant precedent for employment-context FRS use.
